steps.title: Extract data from histogram and calculate the original mean
steps.content:
From the histogram, count the number of salmon in each weight interval:
  • 10–15 pounds: 3 salmon
  • 15–20 pounds: 6 salmon
  • 20–25 pounds: 2 salmon
  • 25–30 pounds: 1 salmon
  • 30–35 pounds: 2 salmon
To calculate the mean, use the midpoint of each interval: $$\text{Mean} = \frac{3(12.5) + 6(17.5) + 2(22.5) + 1(27.5) + 2(32.5)}{14} = \frac{37.5 + 105 + 45 + 27.5 + 65}{14} = \frac{280}{14} = 20$$The original mean is 20 pounds.

steps.title: Find the original median and determine if it changes when 68 is added
steps.content:
With 14 salmon, the median is the average of the 7th and 8th values when ordered. Counting through the intervals:
  • Positions 1–3: 12.5 pounds
  • Positions 4–9: 17.5 pounds
  • Positions 10–11: 22.5 pounds
  • Position 12: 27.5 pounds
  • Positions 13–14: 32.5 pounds
The 7th and 8th values are both 17.5, so the original median is 17.5 pounds.

When 68 pounds is added, the new data set has 15 values. The median becomes the 8th value. Since 68 is much larger than every other weight, it goes to the end of the ordered list and does not affect which value occupies position 8. The 8th value remains 17.5 pounds, so the median does not increase.

steps.title: Determine if the mean increases when 68 is added
steps.content:
When a new value is added to a data set, the mean increases if and only if that new value is greater than the original mean.

Here, the original mean is 20 pounds and the added weight is 68 pounds.

Since $68 > 20$, the new mean becomes: $$\text{New mean} = \frac{280 + 68}{15} = \frac{348}{15} = 23.2 \text{ pounds}$$The mean increases from 20 to 23.2 pounds.

steps.title: Identify which measures must increase
steps.content:
• I. The median: stays at 17.5 pounds—does NOT increase
  • II. The mean: increases from 20 to 23.2 pounds—DOES increase
Only the mean must be greater for the new data set. That matches choice B — B is correct.
